From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [British India](/w/index.php?title=British_India&redirect=no "British India"))

1612–1947 British directly-ruled administrative divisions in India

"British India" redirects here. For the history of the British Empire on the Indian subcontinent, see [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"). For the British East India Company's rule in India, see [Company rule in India](/wiki/Company_rule_in_India "Company rule in India"). For other uses, see [British India (disambiguation)](/wiki/British_India_(disambiguation) "British India (disambiguation)").

For the modern states, see [States and union territories of India](/wiki/States_and_union_territories_of_India "States and union territories of India").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Map_of_the_political_divisions_of_British_India%2C_published_in_the_%27Imperial_Gazetteer_of_India%27_%28Vol._XXVI%2C_Atlas%3B_1931_revised_edition%3B_plate_no._21%29.jpg/330px-Map_of_the_political_divisions_of_British_India%2C_published_in_the_%27Imperial_Gazetteer_of_India%27_%28Vol._XXVI%2C_Atlas%3B_1931_revised_edition%3B_plate_no._21%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_political_divisions_of_British_India,_published_in_the_%27Imperial_Gazetteer_of_India%27_(Vol._XXVI,_Atlas;_1931_revised_edition;_plate_no._21).jpg)

Map of British-administered India (pink) and Indian [princely states](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state") (yellow) in 1931

The **provinces of India**, earlier '**presidencies of British India**', and still earlier '**presidency towns**', were the administrative divisions directly administered by the British authorities that formed part of the system of [British governance on the Indian subcontinent](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"). Collectively, they have been called **British India**. In one form or another, they existed between 1612 and 1947, conventionally divided into three historical periods:

* Between 1612 and 1757, the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") ('Company' or 'EIC') set up ["factories" (trading posts)](/wiki/Factory_(trading_post) "Factory (trading post)") in several locations, mostly in coastal India, with the consent of the [Mughal emperors](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire"), [Maratha Empire](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire") or other local rulers. EIC rivals at this time were the merchant trading companies of Portugal, Denmark, the Netherlands, and France. By the mid-18th century three *presidency towns*: [Madras (Chennai)](/wiki/Madras "Madras"), [Bombay (Mumbai)](/wiki/Bombay "Bombay") and [Calcutta (Kolkata)](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta"), had grown in size.
* During the period of [Company rule in India](/wiki/Company_rule_in_India "Company rule in India") (1757–1858), the Company gradually acquired sovereignty over large parts of India, typically thought of in terms of polities (political areas) called "presidencies". However, EIC itself had also increasingly come under British government oversight, in effect sharing its jurisdiction or control over the Indian presidencies with the British Crown (which itself was increasingly controlled by the Westminster Parliament and the Governments to which it gave majority support). During the same period, EIC also gradually lost its mercantile privileges.
* Following the [Indian Rebellion of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857"), the EIC's remaining powers were transferred (back) to the Crown. Under the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") (1858–1947), administrative boundaries were extended to include a few other British-administered regions, such as [Upper Burma](/wiki/Upper_Burma "Upper Burma"). Increasingly, however, the unwieldy presidencies were broken up into 'provinces'.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

"British India" did not include the many [princely states](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state") which continued to be ruled by Indian princes, usually as vassals to the British. However, by the 19th century, such princely states were effectively under British [suzerainty](/wiki/Suzerainty#India "Suzerainty")—their power over defence policies, foreign relations, and external communications had been relinquished to British authorities, which also closely monitored their internal governance.[[2]](#cite_note-2) At the time of [Indian Independence in 1947](/wiki/Indian_Independence_Act_1947 "Indian Independence Act 1947"), there were officially 565 princely states: a few were very large, although most were very small. In total, princely states made up a quarter of the total population of the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") and represented two fifths of its total land area, with the provinces comprising the remainder in each case.[[3]](#cite_note-statesman-yb-1949-1-3)

## British in India (1608–1947)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: British in India (1608–1947)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Fort_William%2C_Calcutta%2C_1735.jpg/250px-Fort_William%2C_Calcutta%2C_1735.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fort_William,_Calcutta,_1735.jpg)

[Fort William](/wiki/Fort_William,_West_Bengal "Fort William, West Bengal"), [Calcutta](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata"), the capital of the [Bengal Presidency](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency") in British India 1735

In 1608, the [Mughal Emperor](/wiki/Mughal_Emperor "Mughal Emperor") [Jahangir](/wiki/Jahangir "Jahangir") issued a royal [firman](/wiki/Firman "Firman") (a type of decree or warrant) to the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") ('EIC' or 'Company') to establish a small trading settlement at [Surat](/wiki/Surat "Surat") (now in the state of [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat")), and this became the company's first headquarters town. It was followed in 1611 by a permanent [factory](/wiki/Factory_(trading_post) "Factory (trading post)") at [Machilipatnam](/wiki/Machilipatnam "Machilipatnam") on the [Coromandel Coast](/wiki/Coromandel_Coast "Coromandel Coast"), and in 1612 the company joined other already established European trading companies in [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal") in trade.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The defeat of the company by 1690 in the [Anglo-Mughal war](/wiki/Anglo-Mughal_war_(1686%E2%80%931690) "Anglo-Mughal war (1686–1690)") resulted in temporary destabilization.[[5]](#cite_note-5) However, the power of the Mughal Empire declined from 1707, first at the hands of the [Marathas](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire") and later due to invasion from Persia (1739) and Afghanistan (1761). After the East India Company's victories at the [Battle of Plassey](/wiki/Battle_of_Plassey "Battle of Plassey") (1757), and [Battle of Buxar](/wiki/Battle_of_Buxar "Battle of Buxar") (1764)—both within the [Bengal Presidency](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency") established in 1765—and the abolition of local rule ('Nizamat') in Bengal in 1793, the company gradually began to formally expand its territories across [India](/wiki/India "India").[[6]](#cite_note-6) By the mid-19th century, and after the three [Anglo-Maratha Wars](/wiki/Anglo-Maratha_Wars_(disambiguation) "Anglo-Maratha Wars (disambiguation)") and the four [Anglo-Mysore Wars](/wiki/Anglo-Mysore_Wars "Anglo-Mysore Wars"), the East India Company had become the paramount political and military power in south Asia, its territory held in [trust](/wiki/Trust_law "Trust law") for the [British Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown").[[7]](#cite_note-igi-ii-pp488-514-7)

Company rule in Bengal (after 1793) was terminated by the [Government of India Act 1858](/wiki/Government_of_India_Act_1858 "Government of India Act 1858"), following the events of the Bengal [Rebellion of 1857](/wiki/Rebellion_of_1857 "Rebellion of 1857").[[7]](#cite_note-igi-ii-pp488-514-7) Henceforth known as British India, it was thereafter directly ruled as a colonial possession of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"), and India was officially known after 1876 as the [Indian Empire](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj").[[8]](#cite_note-8) India was divided into British India, regions that were directly administered by the British, with acts established and passed in the British parliament,[[9]](#cite_note-igi-iv-pp46-57-9) and the [princely states](/wiki/Princely_states "Princely states"),[[10]](#cite_note-10) ruled by local rulers of different ethnic backgrounds. These rulers were allowed a measure of internal autonomy in exchange for recognition of British [suzerainty](/wiki/Suzerainty "Suzerainty"). British India constituted a significant portion of India both in area and population; in 1910, for example, it covered approximately 54% of the area and included over 77% of the population.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In addition, there were [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_India "Portuguese India") and [French](/wiki/French_India "French India") [exclaves](/wiki/Exclave "Exclave") in India. Independence from British rule was achieved in 1947 with the formation of two nations, the [Dominions](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion") of [India](/wiki/Dominion_of_India "Dominion of India") and [Pakistan](/wiki/Dominion_of_Pakistan "Dominion of Pakistan"), the latter including [East Bengal](/wiki/East_Bengal "East Bengal"), present-day [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh").

The term *British India* also applied to [Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma") (now Myanmar) for a shorter time period: beginning in 1824 with a small part of Burma, but by 1886 almost two thirds of Burma had been made part of British India.[[9]](#cite_note-igi-iv-pp46-57-9) This arrangement lasted until 1937, when Burma was [reorganized](/wiki/Separation_of_Burma "Separation of Burma") as a separate British colony. The term *British India* did not apply to other countries in the region, such as [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") (then [Ceylon](/wiki/British_Ceylon "British Ceylon")), which was a British [Crown colony](/wiki/Crown_colony "Crown colony"), nor to the [Maldive Islands](/wiki/Maldive_Islands "Maldive Islands"), which were a British [protectorate](/wiki/Protectorate#Colonial_protection "Protectorate"). At its greatest extent, in the early 20th century, the territory of British India extended as far as the frontiers of [Persia](/wiki/Persia "Persia") in the west; [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") in the northwest; [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") in the north, [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet") in the northeast; and China, [French Indochina](/wiki/French_Indochina "French Indochina") and [Siam](/wiki/Siam "Siam") in the east. It also included the [Aden Province](/wiki/Chief_Commissioner%27s_Province_of_Aden "Chief Commissioner's Province of Aden") in the [Arabian Peninsula](/wiki/Arabian_Peninsula "Arabian Peninsula").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Administration under the East India Company (1793–1858)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Administration under the East India Company (1793–1858)")]

Main article: [Company rule in India](/wiki/Company_rule_in_India "Company rule in India")

The [East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company"), which was incorporated on 31 December 1600, established trade relations with Indian rulers in [Masulipatam](/wiki/Masulipatam "Masulipatam") on the east coast in 1611 and [Surat](/wiki/Surat "Surat") on the west coast in 1612.[[13]](#cite_note-igi4-6-13) The company rented a small trading outpost in [Madras](/wiki/Madras "Madras") in 1639.[[13]](#cite_note-igi4-6-13) Bombay, which was ceded to the British Crown by [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") as part of the wedding dowry of [Catherine of Braganza](/wiki/Catherine_of_Braganza "Catherine of Braganza") in 1661, was in turn granted to the East India Company to be held in trust for the Crown.[[13]](#cite_note-igi4-6-13)

Meanwhile, in [eastern India](/wiki/East_India "East India"), after obtaining permission from the Mughal Emperor [Shah Jahan](/wiki/Shah_Jahan "Shah Jahan") to trade with Bengal, the company established its first factory at [Hoogly](/wiki/Hooghly_(town) "Hooghly (town)") in 1640.[[13]](#cite_note-igi4-6-13) Almost a half-century later, after Mughal Emperor [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb") forced the company out of Hooghly for its tax evasion, [Job Charnock](/wiki/Job_Charnock "Job Charnock") was a tenant of three small villages, later renamed [Calcutta](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta"), in 1686, making it the company's new headquarters.[[13]](#cite_note-igi4-6-13) By the mid-18th century, the three principal trading settlements including factories and forts, were then called the Madras Presidency (or the Presidency of Fort St. George), the Bombay Presidency, and the Bengal Presidency (or the Presidency of Fort William)—each administered by a governor.[[14]](#cite_note-igi4-7-14)

* [Madras Presidency](/wiki/Madras_Presidency "Madras Presidency"): established 1640.
* [Bombay Presidency](/wiki/Bombay_Presidency "Bombay Presidency"): East India Company's headquarters moved from [Surat](/wiki/Surat "Surat") to Bombay (Mumbai) in 1687.
* [Bengal Presidency](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency"): established 1690.

After [Robert Clive](/wiki/Robert_Clive "Robert Clive")'s victory in the [Battle of Plassey](/wiki/Battle_of_Plassey "Battle of Plassey") in 1757, the puppet government of a new [Nawab of Bengal](/wiki/Nawab_of_Bengal "Nawab of Bengal"), was maintained by the East India Company.[[15]](#cite_note-igi4-9-15) However, after the invasion of Bengal by the [Nawab of Oudh](/wiki/Nawab_of_Oudh "Nawab of Oudh") in 1764 and his subsequent defeat in the [Battle of Buxar](/wiki/Battle_of_Buxar "Battle of Buxar"), the Company obtained the *Diwani* of Bengal, which included the right to administer and collect land-revenue (land tax) in *Bengal*, the region of present-day Bangladesh, West Bengal, Jharkhand and [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar") beginning from 1772 as per the treaty signed in 1765.[[15]](#cite_note-igi4-9-15) By 1773, the Company obtained the *Nizāmat* of Bengal (the "exercise of criminal jurisdiction") and thereby full practical sovereignty in the expanded [Bengal Presidency](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency").[[15]](#cite_note-igi4-9-15) During the period, 1773 to 1785, very little changed; the only exceptions were the addition of the dominions of the *[Raja](/wiki/Raja "Raja")* of [Banares](/wiki/Varanasi "Varanasi") to the western boundary of the Bengal Presidency, and the addition of [Salsette Island](/wiki/Salsette_Island "Salsette Island") to the [Bombay Presidency](/wiki/Bombay_Presidency "Bombay Presidency").[[16]](#cite_note-igi4-10-16)

Portions of the [Kingdom of Mysore](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Mysore "Kingdom of Mysore") were annexed to the [Madras Presidency](/wiki/Madras_Presidency "Madras Presidency") after the [Third Anglo-Mysore War](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Mysore_War "Third Anglo-Mysore War") ended in 1792. Next, in 1799, after the defeat of [Tipu Sultan](/wiki/Tipu_Sultan "Tipu Sultan") in the [Fourth Anglo-Mysore War](/wiki/Fourth_Anglo-Mysore_War "Fourth Anglo-Mysore War") more of his territory was annexed to the Madras Presidency.[[16]](#cite_note-igi4-10-16) In 1801, [Carnatic](/wiki/Carnatic_region "Carnatic region"), which had been under the [suzerainty](/wiki/Suzerainty "Suzerainty") of the company, began to be directly administered by it as a part of the Madras Presidency.[[17]](#cite_note-igi4-11-17)

### The new provinces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: The new provinces")]

By 1851, the East India Company's vast and growing holdings across the sub-continent were still grouped into just four main territories:

* [Bengal Presidency](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency") with its capital at [Calcutta](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta").
* [Bombay Presidency](/wiki/Bombay_Presidency "Bombay Presidency") with its capital at [Bombay](/wiki/Bombay "Bombay").
* [Madras Presidency](/wiki/Madras_Presidency "Madras Presidency") with its capital at [Madras](/wiki/Madras "Madras").
* [North-Western Provinces](/wiki/North-Western_Provinces "North-Western Provinces") with the seat of the lieutenant-governor at [Agra](/wiki/Agra "Agra"). The original seat of government was at [Allahabad](/wiki/Allahabad "Allahabad"), then at [Agra](/wiki/Agra "Agra") from 1834 to 1868. In 1833, an [act of the British Parliament](/wiki/Act_of_Parliament_(UK) "Act of Parliament (UK)"), the [Government of India Act 1833](/wiki/Government_of_India_Act_1833 "Government of India Act 1833") ([3 & 4 Will. 4](/wiki/3_%26_4_Will._4 "3 & 4 Will. 4"). c. 85) promulgated the elevation of the *[Ceded and Conquered Provinces](/wiki/Ceded_and_Conquered_Provinces "Ceded and Conquered Provinces")* to the new *Presidency of Agra*, and the appointment of a new governor for the latter, but the plan was never carried out. In 1835 another act of Parliament, the [India (North-West Provinces) Act 1835](/wiki/India_(North-West_Provinces)_Act_1835 "India (North-West Provinces) Act 1835") ([5 & 6 Will. 4](/wiki/5_%26_6_Will._4 "5 & 6 Will. 4"). c. 52) renamed the region the *North-Western Provinces*, this time to be administered by a lieutenant-governor, the first of whom, Sir [Charles Metcalfe](/wiki/Charles_Metcalfe,_1st_Baron_Metcalfe "Charles Metcalfe, 1st Baron Metcalfe"), would be appointed in 1836.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

By the time of the [Indian Rebellion of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857"), and the end of Company rule, the developments could be summarised as follows:

* [Bombay Presidency](/wiki/Bombay_Presidency "Bombay Presidency"): expanded after the [Anglo-Maratha Wars](/wiki/Anglo-Maratha_Wars_(disambiguation) "Anglo-Maratha Wars (disambiguation)").
* [Madras Presidency](/wiki/Madras_Presidency "Madras Presidency"): expanded in the mid-to-late 18th century [Carnatic Wars](/wiki/Carnatic_Wars "Carnatic Wars") and [Anglo-Mysore Wars](/wiki/Anglo-Mysore_Wars "Anglo-Mysore Wars").
* [Bengal Presidency](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency"): expanded after the battles of [Plassey](/wiki/Battle_of_Plassey "Battle of Plassey") (1757) and [Buxar](/wiki/Battle_of_Buxar "Battle of Buxar") (1764), and after the [Second](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Maratha_War "Second Anglo-Maratha War") and [Third Anglo-Maratha Wars](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Maratha_War "Third Anglo-Maratha War").
* [Penang](/wiki/Penang "Penang"): became a residency within the Bengal Presidency in 1786, the fourth presidency of India in 1805, part of the presidency of the [Straits Settlements](/wiki/Straits_Settlements "Straits Settlements") until 1830, again part of a residency within the Bengal Presidency when the [Straits Settlements](/wiki/Straits_Settlements "Straits Settlements") became so, and finally separated from British India in 1867.
* [Ceded and Conquered Provinces](/wiki/Ceded_and_Conquered_Provinces "Ceded and Conquered Provinces"): established in 1802 within the [Bengal Presidency](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency"). Proposed to be renamed the [Presidency of Agra](/wiki/Presidency_of_Agra "Presidency of Agra") under a governor in 1835, but the proposal was not implemented.
* [Ajmer-Merwara](/wiki/Ajmer-Merwara "Ajmer-Merwara"): ceded by [Sindhia](/wiki/Sindhia "Sindhia") of [Gwalior](/wiki/Gwalior "Gwalior") in 1818 at the conclusion of the [Third Anglo-Maratha War](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Maratha_War "Third Anglo-Maratha War").
* [Coorg Province](/wiki/Coorg_Province "Coorg Province"): annexed in 1834.
* [North-Western Provinces](/wiki/North-Western_Provinces "North-Western Provinces"): established as a lieutenant-governorship in 1836 from the erstwhile *Ceded and Conquered Provinces.*
* [Sind](/wiki/Sind_State "Sind State"): annexed to the Bombay Presidency in 1843.
* [Punjab Province](/wiki/Punjab_Province_(British_India) "Punjab Province (British India)"): established in 1849 from territories captured in the [First](/wiki/First_Anglo-Sikh_War "First Anglo-Sikh War") and [Second](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Sikh_War "Second Anglo-Sikh War") Anglo-Sikh Wars.
* [Nagpur Province](/wiki/Nagpur_Province "Nagpur Province"): created in 1853 from the princely state of Nagpur, seized by the [doctrine of lapse](/wiki/Doctrine_of_lapse "Doctrine of lapse"), then merged into the Central Provinces in 1861.
* [Oudh State](/wiki/Oudh_State "Oudh State"): annexed in 1856 and governed thereafter until 1905 as a 'chief commissionership', as a part of [North-Western Provinces](/wiki/North-Western_Provinces "North-Western Provinces") and Oudh.

## Administration under the Crown (1858–1947)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Administration under the Crown (1858–1947)")]

Main article: [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj")

### Historical background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Historical background")]

The British Raj began with the idea of the presidencies as the centres of government. Until 1834, when a General Legislative Council was formed, each presidency under its governor and council was empowered to enact a code of so-called 'regulations' for its government. Therefore, any territory or province that was added by conquest or treaty to a presidency came under the existing regulations of the corresponding presidency. However, in the case of provinces that were acquired but were not annexed to any of the three presidencies, their official staff could be provided as the governor-general pleased, and was not governed by the existing regulations of the Bengal, Madras, or Bombay presidencies. Such provinces became known as non-regulation provinces and up to 1833 no provision for a legislative power existed in such places.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] The same two kinds of management applied for districts. Thus [Ganjam](/wiki/Ganjam_district "Ganjam district") and [Vizagapatam](/wiki/Visakhapatnam_district "Visakhapatnam district") were non-regulation districts.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Non-regulation provinces included:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [Ajmer Province](/wiki/Ajmer-Merwara "Ajmer-Merwara") (Ajmer-Merwara)
* [Cis-Sutlej states](/wiki/Cis-Sutlej_states "Cis-Sutlej states")
* [Saugor and Nerbudda Territories](/wiki/Saugor_and_Nerbudda_Territories "Saugor and Nerbudda Territories")
* [North-East Frontier](/wiki/North-East_Frontier "North-East Frontier")
* [Cooch Behar state](/wiki/Cooch_Behar_state "Cooch Behar state")
* [South-West Frontier](/wiki/South-West_Frontier "South-West Frontier") (Chota Nagpur)
* [Jhansi State](/wiki/Jhansi_State "Jhansi State")
* [Kumaon](/wiki/Kumaon_Province "Kumaon Province")

### Regulation provinces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Regulation provinces")]

* [Central Provinces](/wiki/Central_Provinces_and_Berar "Central Provinces and Berar"): Created in 1861 from Nagpur Province and the [Saugor and Nerbudda Territories](/wiki/Saugor_and_Nerbudda_Territories "Saugor and Nerbudda Territories"). Berar was added to the province in 1903, and was renamed the Central Provinces and Berar in 1936.
* [Burma](/wiki/British_rule_in_Burma "British rule in Burma"): [Lower Burma](/wiki/Lower_Burma "Lower Burma") annexed 1852, established as a province in 1862, [Upper Burma](/wiki/Upper_Burma "Upper Burma") incorporated in 1886. Separated from British India in 1937 to become administered independently by the newly established British Government [Burma Office](/wiki/Burma_Office "Burma Office").
* [Assam](/wiki/Colonial_Assam "Colonial Assam"): separated from Bengal in 1874 as the [North-East Frontier](/wiki/North-East_Frontier "North-East Frontier") non-regulation province. Incorporated into the new province of [Eastern Bengal and Assam](/wiki/Eastern_Bengal_and_Assam "Eastern Bengal and Assam") in 1905. Re-established as a province in 1912.
* [Andaman and Nicobar Islands](/wiki/Andaman_and_Nicobar_Islands "Andaman and Nicobar Islands"): established as a province in 1875.
* [Baluchistan](/wiki/Baluchistan_(Chief_Commissioner%27s_Province) "Baluchistan (Chief Commissioner's Province)"): Organised into a province in 1887.

* [North-West Frontier Province](/wiki/North-West_Frontier_Province "North-West Frontier Province"): created in 1901 from the north-western districts of Punjab Province.
* [Eastern Bengal and Assam](/wiki/Eastern_Bengal_and_Assam "Eastern Bengal and Assam"): created in 1905 upon the partition of Bengal, together with the former province of Assam. Re-merged with Bengal in 1912, with north-eastern part re-established as the province of Assam.

* [Sind](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh"): Separated from Bombay in 1936.

## Partition and independence (1947)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Partition and independence (1947)")]

At the time of independence in 1947, British India had 17 provinces:

* [Ajmer-Merwara](/wiki/Ajmer-Merwara "Ajmer-Merwara")
* [Andaman and Nicobar Islands](/wiki/Andaman_and_Nicobar_Islands "Andaman and Nicobar Islands")
* [Assam](/wiki/Assam_Province "Assam Province")
* [Baluchistan](/wiki/Baluchistan_(Chief_Commissioner%27s_Province) "Baluchistan (Chief Commissioner's Province)")
* [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency")
* [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar_Province "Bihar Province")
* [Bombay](/wiki/Bombay_Presidency "Bombay Presidency")
* [Central Provinces and Berar](/wiki/Central_Provinces_and_Berar "Central Provinces and Berar")
* [Coorg](/wiki/Coorg_Province "Coorg Province")
* [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi")
* [Madras](/wiki/Madras_Presidency "Madras Presidency")
* [North-West Frontier](/wiki/North-West_Frontier_Province_(1901%E2%80%931955) "North-West Frontier Province (1901–1955)")
* [Orissa](/wiki/Orissa_Province "Orissa Province")
* [Panth Piploda](/wiki/Panth_Piploda_Province "Panth Piploda Province")
* [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab_Province_(British_India) "Punjab Province (British India)")
* [Sind](/wiki/Sind_Province_(1936%E2%80%931955) "Sind Province (1936–1955)")
* [United Provinces](/wiki/United_Provinces_(1937%E2%80%931950) "United Provinces (1937–1950)")

Upon the [partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_British_India "Partition of British India") into the [Dominion of India](/wiki/Dominion_of_India "Dominion of India") and [Dominion of Pakistan](/wiki/Dominion_of_Pakistan "Dominion of Pakistan"), eleven provinces (Ajmer-Merwara-Kekri, Andaman and Nicobar Islands, Bihar, Bombay, Central Provinces and Berar, Coorg, Delhi, Madras, Panth Piploda, Orissa, and the United Provinces) joined India, three (Baluchistan, North-West Frontier and Sindh) joined Pakistan, and three ([Punjab](/wiki/British_Punjab "British Punjab"), [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal") and [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam")) were partitioned between India and Pakistan.

In 1950, after the new [Indian constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_India "Constitution of India") was adopted, the provinces in India were replaced by redrawn states and union territories. Pakistan, however, retained its five provinces, one of which, [East Bengal](/wiki/East_Bengal "East Bengal"), was renamed [East Pakistan](/wiki/East_Pakistan "East Pakistan") in 1956 and became the independent nation of [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") in 1971.
